critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed experimental design. The chronological split with campaign-deduplication clustering is the right call for adversarial email, and the explicit exclusion of ambiguous labels is methodologically honest. Preprocessing discipline (fit on train only, reputation snapshotted at receipt time) is correctly specified. The baseline is genuinely strong rather than a strawman. Let me work through the places where I think real problems remain.

**1. The label construction process is the central assumption, and it has a systematic bias that corrupts both the positive and negative classes.**

The claim: analyst/incident disposition constitutes a reliable ground-truth oracle for the positive class, and explicit benign resolution constitutes a reliable oracle for the negative class.

Why this is wrong in a specific way: Analyst review and incident closure are not independent of the signals the model will use. Emails that look obviously malicious — strong URL heuristics, known-bad domains, SPF failures — are triaged first and closed as confirmed malicious faster. Emails that are genuinely novel or use clean infrastructure and legitimate-looking text are harder to triage, more likely to remain unresolved, and are therefore excluded from training by the 'ambiguous/unresolved' filter. The positive training set is thus systematically biased toward the easy, already-detectable positives. Meanwhile, the negative class is drawn from messages explicitly cleared as benign, which again over-represents high-confidence cases. The model trains on a joint distribution that is easier than the actual operational distribution. PR-AUC on the held-out test set will be inflated because the test set suffers the same selection bias — novel or stealthy phishes in the test window that were unresolved are also excluded. The model is evaluated on the cases the analyst pipeline already handles, not the full threat surface.

What would constitute evidence: Measure the time-to-disposition distribution for confirmed positives. If the median time is short (hours) and the tail is long, and if the label exclusion rate is substantial (say, >15% of suspicious-flagged messages remain unresolved), that is evidence of selection bias. Alternatively, compare model scores on the excluded 'ambiguous' set against the confirmed sets — if ambiguous messages score intermediate rather than high, that confirms the model is not generalizing to the hard cases. A retroactive labeling exercise on a random sample of excluded messages (using additional threat intel lookups applied after the test window) would directly measure how many were true positives.

**2. Campaign deduplication by shingle similarity is underspecified in a way that could either over- or under-cluster, and either failure mode breaks the comparison.**

The claim: near-duplicate clustering via normalized subject/body/URL shingles assigns template variants to a single split, preventing leakage.

Why this might fail: Phishing campaigns vary significantly in how they mutate. Highly polymorphic campaigns — which rotate body text, randomize URLs, and spoof different sender domains per message — produce low shingle similarity between instances and will not be clustered together. These campaign variants will appear in both train and test, causing leakage of exactly the kind the clustering is intended to prevent. Conversely, legitimate mass mailers (newsletters, service notifications) may produce very high shingle similarity and be incorrectly assigned to a single split, reducing the effective training or test size and degrading statistical power. The deduplication threshold is a hyperparameter that needs to be validated against known campaign ground truth, but no such validation procedure is specified.

What would constitute evidence: Apply the shingle clustering to a corpus with known campaign labels (e.g., from threat-intel tagging of campaigns by family). Measure whether instances of the same campaign family are assigned to the same cluster at the chosen threshold. Measure the false-positive rate (legitimate mail clusters incorrectly merged). If campaign recall at the cluster level is below ~90% for known polymorphic campaigns, the leakage protection is insufficient. The threshold should be selected based on this validation, not chosen heuristically.

**3. The transformer's text encoder requires that body text and subject lines carry discriminative signal that is not already captured by the structured features — this is a prerequisite assumption, not an evaluation metric, and it is not tested.**

The claim: the multimodal transformer's text representation provides additional discriminative power over and above what the GBT's n-gram summaries and heuristics capture.

Why this prerequisite might not hold: In enterprise phishing, a large fraction of detectable malicious emails are detectable from structured signals alone — authentication failures, known-bad URLs, reputation buckets, attachment metadata. The text signals matter most for the residual hard cases: socially engineered emails using clean infrastructure and convincing prose. If the positive training set is dominated by easy positives (per issue 1), the transformer's text encoder receives little gradient signal specifically from the hard cases. The model may learn to weight structured features heavily and effectively ignore the textual representation for the cases where it would matter. The ablation proposed (removing length and formatting features) does not test this — it removes different features. The correct ablation is to compare the full transformer against a transformer that receives only text (no structured features) versus the GBT that receives only structured features, to isolate where the gains actually originate.

What would constitute evidence: Run an ablation where the structured feature input to the transformer is zeroed out or replaced with noise. If PR-AUC drops substantially, the text encoder is contributing. If PR-AUC is approximately maintained by structured features alone, the transformer's text modeling is not the source of improvement. Additionally, inspect attention patterns or SHAP-equivalent attributions on the hard positive cases (high score, ambiguous features) to verify text is driving those rankings.

**4. Reputation snapshotting 'as of receipt time' is correct in principle but may be infeasible in practice in a way that introduces silent leakage.**

The claim: sender/domain reputation values reflect only information available at receipt time.

The mechanism of failure: Reputation feeds (IP blacklists, domain age registries, URL reputation services) are typically provided as current snapshots, not as point-in-time historical records. To reconstruct reputation as of a message's receipt timestamp one year ago requires that the organization archived reputation query responses at ingest time. If they did not, reputation must be recomputed from current data or from periodic snapshots, both of which leak future information: a domain that was clean at receipt time but later blocklisted will appear as a high-reputation-risk domain in the reconstructed feature, correctly labeling a phish but using information that did not exist at decision time. This inflates the apparent performance of reputation-dependent features and may give the GBT (which relies more heavily on reputation) an unfair advantage, or may inflate both models' performance relative to true deployment conditions.

What would constitute evidence: Audit whether reputation values were logged at message receipt time in the mail gateway or SIEM. If not, characterize what fraction of confirmed-positive domains were clean at receipt but blocklisted within the following 30/90 days — this is the contamination rate. If that rate exceeds a few percent, reputation features should be excluded from the primary comparison and reported as a separate ablation only.

In summary: issues 1 and 4 are the most likely to silently inflate reported performance relative to true deployment performance. Issue 2 could cause leakage that specifically inflates transformer performance (if campaigns that use similar templates are the ones the transformer excels at, and those templates appear in both train and test). Issue 3 is a prerequisite verification gap that could cause the experiment to conclude in favor of the transformer for the wrong reason. None of these are fatal to the experimental design as a framework, but issues 1 and 4 in particular need to be addressed before the PR-AUC numbers can be trusted as deployment-predictive.
all_issues_raised: Label construction is biased toward easy positives because analyst disposition speed correlates with signal obviousness, causing the excluded 'ambiguous' set to contain the hard cases the model most needs to learn from.

Shingle-based campaign deduplication is an underspecified hyperparameter that fails silently for polymorphic campaigns (low shingle similarity between instances of the same campaign) and may incorrectly merge legitimate mass mail.

The transformer's text encoder contribution is a prerequisite assumption that is not directly tested; the proposed ablations do not isolate whether the text modality drives gains on the hard cases where it would matter.

Reputation snapshotting as of receipt time may be infeasible without archived gateway logs, causing silent future-information leakage if reputation is reconstructed from current or periodic-snapshot data.
